ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dry soba noodles; SNOMED CT coverage of specific ethnic noodle products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of dry soba noodles is approximately 80 g (one bundle), which yields roughly 160–180 g of cooked noodles after boiling.

nutrient_source.title: Soba, dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169726)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) and secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sugar, saturated fat) are drawn from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dry soba (FDC ID 169726). Sodium, potassium, calcium, and iron values are retained from the Singapore HPB Foodo database entry (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95) as the HPB value is the primary regional source for this record and is consistent with USDA ranges. Cholesterol is confirmed 0 mg per both sources. Micronutrients not reported by either source are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 46 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for buckwheat noodles (soba), which is the closest approved analogous entry. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (46) × available carbohydrate per serving (74.62 g × 0.80 serving fraction = 59.7 g) / 100 = 27.5, rounded to 27.6. This reflects the uncooked state; cooked soba has approximately 25 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a GL of approximately 11.5 per 100 g cooked portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 792 mg per 100 g in uncooked state exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Boil soba in unsalted water and rinse thoroughly after cooking, which reduces sodium to approximately 60–100 mg per 100 g cooked; avoid high-sodium tsuyu dipping sauces that can add a further 400–600 mg sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate density of 74.6 g per 100 g in uncooked state; GI 46 (low) but high carbohydrate load per dry weight
type2_diabetes.guidance: Limit dry soba to an 80 g portion (approximately 160 g cooked, yielding ~40 g available carbohydrate), pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein, and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces; the buckwheat-derived rutin may modestly improve insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium in uncooked form; low fat (0.71 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and buckwheat polyphenols (rutin, quercetin) are cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Prepare soba by boiling in unsalted water and rinsing to reduce sodium to safe levels; the low saturated fat content and buckwheat-derived rutin support cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Buckwheat provides rutin and quercetin (anti-inflammatory polyphenols); high sodium in uncooked form requires mitigation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boil in unsalted water and rinse before consuming; choose soba with higher buckwheat content (above 40%) to maximise anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit and avoid high-sodium tsuyu sauces that may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese soba (そば, dry/uncooked form) is a traditional Japanese noodle made primarily from buckwheat flour (sobako) blended with wheat flour, formed into thin grey-brown strands and dried. In the uncooked state, soba has a firm, dense texture and a characteristic nutty, earthy aroma derived from buckwheat. It is prepared by boiling in unsalted water for 4–6 minutes, then rinsing under cold water to remove surface starch and excess sodium. Soba is served either chilled with a dipping sauce (zaru soba) or hot in a dashi-based broth (kake soba). Nutritionally, dry soba is energy-dense at 336 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 14.4 g per 100 g — notably higher than most wheat noodles — and a low fat content of 0.71 g per 100 g. Carbohydrate density is 74.6 g per 100 g in the dry state, falling to approximately 25 g per 100 g after cooking due to water absorption. Dietary fibre is 3.5 g per 100 g. The glycemic index of soba is approximately 46 (low), attributed to the buckwheat fraction which slows glucose absorption. Sodium is high at 792 mg per 100 g in the uncooked product due to salt added during manufacture; boiling and rinsing reduces this substantially to 60–100 mg per 100 g cooked. Buckwheat contains rutin and quercetin, polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory and potential insulin-sensitising properties. Clinically, uncooked soba is unsuitable for hypertension management due to its sodium content, but cooked and rinsed soba is appropriate. For type 2 diabetes, portion control is essential given the carbohydrate density. Soba is suitable for plant-based and high-protein dietary patterns and is a culturally significant staple in Japanese cuisine.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in soba noodles?
What is the GI of soba noodles?
Can I eat soba if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between soba and udon?
How many calories in dry soba noodles?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169726) for dry soba. Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, saturated fat, sugar) drawn from USDA SR Legacy. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. All values are consistent across sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for dry soba not reported in Singapore HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this product. water_g set to -1: moisture content for dry soba not reported in the primary sources consulted. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry and are not available in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this specific soba product. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dry soba; no hydrogenated fats used in standard soba manufacture. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy; soba is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived cholesterol. Cultural metadata correction applied: input record listed primary_cuisine as 'western' — corrected to 'Japanese' as soba is a traditional Japanese noodle product. Sodium of 792 mg/100 g reflects the uncooked/dry product; boiling and rinsing reduces sodium to approximately 60–100 mg/100 g in the cooked product. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for buckwheat noodles (soba); glycemic load per serving calculated for an 80 g dry serving (59.7 g available carbohydrate × GI 46 / 100 = 27.5, reported as 27.6).
